info: Cologix, a Denver based company, is in the process of constructing two 135 square foot data centers in Lewis Center, Ohio. Known as COL6 and COL5, Cologix has invested upwards of $1 billion into the project. COL5 is currently under construction and COL6 is planned.

name: Strata Expanse AI Center of Excellence
info: Strata Expanse has announced a partnership with RAVEL and Supermicro to construct a data center. This campus will be unique in that instead of directly linking to the local power grid, it will use a combination of different power sources, including natural gas, geothermal energy, cooling, hydrogen, energy storage, and solar energy to operate.

name: Hannibal Data Center Campus
info: This proposed data center is planned to be built succeeding the construction of a 485 MW natural gas power plant in a deal between DP Facilities and Long Ridge Energy, subsidiary of Fortress Transportation and Infrastructure Investors LLC. Long Ridge Energy plans to allocate about 125 acres for data center development. DP Facilities is citing it will only occupy 30 acres while Long ridge indicates the entire campus will have a capacity of 300 MW. The exact capacity has not been said on any press releases. Along with the colocated plant, the site takes advantage of existing 138kV transmission lines.

name: Bitdeer Clarington Data Center
info: Bitdeer is developing a 570 MW, high-performance computing (HPC) and AI-ready data center at the Hannibal Industrial Park. However, the developer is being sued by neighboring tenant American Heavy Plate Solutions, who claims the data center would disrupt shared infrastructure guaranteed under its long-term lease at the Hannibal Industrial Park. The landlord, Monroe County Port Authority, is also named in the suit. AHP argues Bitdeer’s 30-year lease and planned 570-MW development violate a restrictive covenant requiring tenant consent for any project that negatively affects its operations. Bitdeer had targeted completion around mid 2027, but now says timing is uncertain due to the lawsuit. Information will be updated as it is released.

name: Meta Bowling Green Data Center
address: East of Route 25 and North of Route 582
info: Meta came out with a public annoucnement that it will soon be operating a data center being built in Middle County, Ohio, north of Bowling Green. A Meta-owned entity called Liams LLC purchased more than 750 acres across 5 parcels located on both sides of Route 582, near I-75. Acoording to the Wood County Geolibrary, the most recent land purchases came in April 2025, and current satelite imagery shows that this land, around 160 acres, is what is being developed at this time. Meta has invested roughly $800 million in total to build the data center, which includes the cost of the land. The site was granted a 75% tax abatement when it was originally approved. Second hand reports of the Wood County Comissioners meeting where this took place reveals there are two buildings, each up to 400,000 square feet beign built.

name: NEO-01 / Sandusky Data Center
info: Aligned Data Centers is constructing a 300,000-square-foot data center on 129 acres at the former KBI/Delphi site. The project broke ground in 2024 and is being built in phases, with completion expected in 2026.

name: Microsoft Heath Data Center
info: Microsoft is constructing a data center off Thornwood Drive in Heath, as part of a $1 billion investment in Ohio.

name: Microsoft Hebron Data Center
info: Microsoft is constructing a data center off High Street in Hebron. Microsoft originally delayed this decision, but finally broke ground in early 2026.

name: Microsoft New Albany Data Center
info: Microsoft purchased 197 acres of land from Licking Country on March 12th, 2024 off Beech Road near Highway 62. Although research yielded no public records from the Licking County Board of Comissioners or the New Albany Planning Comission approving building plans for Microsoft Corporation on this plot, Microsoft itself has revealed that it does intend to build a data center there. The site currently has no reported capacity. Supposedly, Microsoft received a 100% tax abatement from the New Albany City Council and the company is investing $450 million in the project.

name: Urbana Data Center
address: Corner of State Route 55 and U.S. Route 68, adjacent to the Rittal facility
info: Thor Equities is considering developing a $1 billion data center adjacent to the Rittal LLC Manufacturing Facility.

name: Akron Data Center Expansion
info: ark data centers, LLC, has announced plans to invest more than $136 million to expand data center processing capabilities at its existing locations in Akron and Independence, in addition to a $4.5 million tax break awarded by the Ohio Tax Credit Authority at the recommendation of JobsOhio. This investment is anticipated to create only 10 jobs.

name: AWS Wilmington Data Center
info: AWS has been temporarily blocked by a court order in Clinton Country Ohio, off US 68 S, south Wilmington. The land encompasses 470 acres of rezoned farm land, now set to light industrial. Amazon purchased the land in on December 10th, 2025 for $86.4 million dollars and plans to invest upwards of $4 billion in the project, spending $25 million alone for water and sewage infrustructure updates (2.5 miles of new water main and 3.5 miles of trunk sewer). A site plan presentation took place in a Planning Comission meeting on January 6th, 2026. The comission made a unanimous vote to delay the proposal during this meeting, citing the developer representative's reluctance to provide definitive information regarding water consumption, diesel generator usage, and noise impact among other items. The power consumption has also not been disclosed, but this site falls in line with AES Ohio's Fayette-Clinton 345kV transmission line project, set to roll out by 2030. The plan presentation shows 9 main buildings, a 1 million gallon backup water tower, and a new substation on the property. Plan developers include Arcadis and Bohler. The original proposal was provided by the Clinton County Port Authority during a Wilmington city council meeting on November 25th, 2025.

name: Mt. Orab Mega Site
address: South of Tri County Highway and North of Oakland Road
info: A company going under the name DB STU LLC has purchased nearly 1200 acres of land in Mt. Orab from December of 2024 to January of 2025. This came after a rezoning ordinance passed in December 2024 by the Village of Mt. Orab Council, rezoning the dubbed "Mega Cite" to heavy industrial. This site, plus another parcel bought on the other side of New Hope-White Oak Station Road brings the total land up to around 1150 acres. The village cites that electric and gas are provided by Duke Energy, water and sewer by the Village of Mt. Orab itself, and fiber through Spectrum, Horizon, and Frontier. The exact location of the site is south of Tri County Highway and north of Oakland Road in Mt. Orab. No meeting minutes were found to confirm the exact rezoning dates. Council members have signed NDAs and are not allowed to disclose plans to the residents.

name: J.M. Stuart Revitilization Project
companies: Commercial Liability; Independent Investor; Core Real Estate; High Table LLC; Energy Strike LLC; Hecate Energy
info: A data center project located on the site of a decomisssioned power plant, formally J.M Stuart, off the Ohio river in Sprigg Township originally went offline in 2018, leaving behind spent land requiring revilitization. Commercial Liability purchased the land at the end of 2019 and developed it into usable commercial land. It was purchased in parts since 2023 by Energy Strike, LLC, and was developed simultaneously with the nearby Killen Revitilization Project by High Table, LLC (both owned by the same entity). Water usage is unknown, but the developer has cited "massive" amounts with the possibility of acquiring permits to take from the Ohio River. Roughly 50 acres were sold as options to a Chicago based renewable energy company, Hecate Energy, to build power infrastructure for the site. The exact contribution from this provider is unknown. In a meeting late February, Sprigg Township passed a 12-month data center moratorium following negative feedback from the public. Capcity for this is not certain, but the Killen developer advertises up to 650 MW of power generation can be supplied within 24 months. This estimate is also applied in thsi case on the basis these are sister sites.

name: Killen Revitilization Project
companies: Commercial Liability; Independent Investor; Core Real Estate; High Table LLC; Energy Strike LLC; Hecate Energy
info: A data center project is located on the site of a decomisssioned power plant, formally Killen Generating Station, off the Ohio river in Monroe Township. The sister site is ran by Energy Strike LLC, while this data center is owned by High Table, LLC (both are owned by the same parent entity). The plant originally went offline in 2018. It was purchased soon after by Commercial Liability in 2019 and developed it into usable commercial land. It was purchased in parts since 2023 by High Table. This developer advertises up to 650 MW of power generation can be supplied within 24 months. Water usage is unknown, but the developer has cited "massive" amounts with the possibility of acquiring permits to take from the Ohio River. Roughly 40 acres were sold as options to a Chicago based renewable energy company, Hecate Energy, to build power infrastructure for the site. The exact contribution from this provider is unknown.

name: Marysville South Data Center
companies: EMH&T; New Albany Company; Anadonna LLC; Marysville Land Company LLC; 14879 Industrial Parkway LLC; Weaver Road Holdings LLC; Underhill & Hodge LLC; Mary Ann Vickner / Rosemary A. Emswiler Trust; Amazon Web Services; AES Ohio; Columbia Gas of Ohio; Union Rural Electric
info: The Marysville South site has roots going back to mid-2024, when the New Albany Company (through its affiliate The Marysville Land Company LLC) orchestrated the rezoning of ~591.6 acres from industrial/manufacturing zoning to a Planned Unit Development, with data centers listed as a permitted use by right. Infrastructure is already in place including an AES 138kV transmission line and access to existing water and sewer mains on Scottslawn Road and Industrial Parkway. Acting as master land developer, the New Albany Company then facilitated the CRA agreement passed by Marysville City Council on November 10th, 2025, authorizing Anadonna LLC to develop a data center on the site. The deal includes a 15-year 100% tax exemption on new structures and a PILOT (pay in lieu of taxes) payment plan back to the city, valued at ~$16 million over the 15 years. By January 2026, Anadonna LLC had purchased a 300-acre plot at 14300 Weaver Road for $205 million, the majority of the 591 acre planned site. Images from the City of Marysville's original PUD development plan suggests parcels owned by 14879 Industrial Parkway LLC and The Marysville Land Company II LLC will be absorbed into Anadonna's current ownership, consistent with the original PUD boundary. On March 11th, 2026, Amazon revealed it is the end user, and that it plans to invest upwards of $1 billion in the site by 2030 with operations beginning in 2027, but these numbers might apply to the general area, which includes Project Cosmos on the other side of the Industrial Parkway, also planed to be operated by AWS. AES will provide the site with up to 800 MW by 2033 according to a regulatory filing from February 2026, with the developer estimating 16% renewable usage and committing to remain under 90% of maximum grid capacity.

name: Buck Canyon Properties Data Center
address: East of Ginger Ridge Road and West of US Route 52
companies: Ramboll; 68 Yards LLC; Buck Canyon Properties LLC; AES Ohio; AEP Ohio; Adams County; Sprigg Township; U.S. Army Corps of Engineers; Ohio EPA
info: The Buck Canyon data center project sits on a former Dayton Power & Light landfill off U.S. Route 52 in Manchester, Sprigg Township, Adams County, Ohio. The parcel is 1,016 acres purchased in 2024 for $2.65 million by Buck Canyon Properties LLC. The development entity is 68 Yards LLC, a Chicago-based company, with the end tenant still undisclosed under NDA. AES Ohio's February 3, 2026 PJM regulatory filing confirmed power demand ramping from 100 MW in late 2028 to 1,300 MW by March 2032, which would make it one of Ohio's largest data centers at 31 times the county's entire annual energy consumption, drawing from the existing 345kV infrastructure at the Stuart Substation. Notably, while AES Ohio owns the Stuart Substation, the surrounding plant site falls within AEP's service territory, adding grid coordination complexity that PJM must resolve before full interconnection can proceed. A federal NWP 39 permit was issued February 18, 2026 authorizing stream and wetland impacts for construction. This permit was key for the developers to break ground on the project and begin building infrastructure.

name: PORTS Technology Center
companies: U.S. Department of Energy; SB Energy; SoftBank; AEP Ohio
info: As part of the U.S.–Japan Strategic Trade and Investment Agreement, a strategic initiative announced by the Trump Administration in July 2025, SB Energy, a U.S.-based SoftBank affiliate, announced a partnership with the U.S. Department of Energy in March 2026 to construct the "PORTS Technology Campus": a massive 10 GW AI data center and co-located 9.2 GW natural gas plant, representing a $33 billion investment in generation alone, to be built on the former Portsmouth Gaseous Diffusion Plant site in Pike County, near Piketon. The project is expected to create over 10,000 construction jobs and more than 2,000 permanent operational positions, with GE Vernova supplying approximately 170 gas turbines in a contract valued at roughly $10 billion, the first of which is expected to be delivered within a year. SoftBank, through SB Energy, will also work with American Electric Power (AEP) Ohio to build the transmission infrastructure, representing a separate $4.2 billion investment in new high-voltage lines and substations, which SB Energy has committed to fund directly, with officials stating it will not raise customer electricity rates.

name: Project BOSC
address: 1108 W. Bluelick Road and North Cole Street
info: Google is awaiting approval for a data center in northern Lima, representing a $500 milllion investment.

name: Perry Township Data Center
companies: Panattoni Development Company; AEP Ohio; City of Canton Water and Sewer
info: A data center has been proposed in Perry Township in a commercially zoned parcel of land encompassing 100 acres, and is projected to require 240 MW of power. The developers, Panattoni Development, made a statement in February that AEP will be upgrading both offsite and onsite electrical infrastructure for the project. Water will be managed by the City of Canton. Four 35-foot buildings will house the data center, totalling 756,000 square feet. The site is expected to be complete in April 2028.

name: Morabito Slavic Village Data Center
address: Between East 49th and East 55th, east of Highway 77
info: The City of Cleveland has rejected a permit application from Lakeland Equity Group to build a $1.6 billion data center campus on 35 acres in Slavic Village, between East 49th and East 55th.

name: AWS Salem Township Data Center
address: Adjacent to Susquehanna Steam Electric Station
info: Amazon Web Services is constructing a hyperscale data center campus next to Talen Energy’s Susquehanna Steam Electric Station (nuclear power plant). To power this complex, AWS has entered a $18 billion agreement with Talen Energy that will last through 2042. Capacity will be upgraded over time, initially delivering 840 MW, and then to 1,200 MW in 2029, 1,680 MW, and finally 1,920 MW in 2032.  This investment represents one of the largest private investments in Pennsylvania.

name: Project Hazelnut
address: Humboldt Industrial Park North near PA 924 & I‑81
info: NorthPoint Development of Kansas City plans Project Hazelnut, a 15-building hyperscale data center on a roughly 1,250-acre former mine site in Hazle Township, using enough power for 90,000 homes and cooled with sewer water. The project, part of Pennsylvania’s Fast Track program, is projected to create 900 permanent jobs but faces public opposition over power-line and tax-break issues. NorthPoint has already built a 1-million-square-foot warehouse nearby and now owns over 1,500 acres in the area. As of mid-November, the Hazle Township Board of Supervisors unanimously rejected the land application that would have enabled the project's construction. Developers still have have ability to appeal within 30 days, hence this proposal has been marked as rejected.

name: Wildcat Ridge AI Data Center
info: Cornell Realty Management LLC is proposing a ~$2.1 billion data center campus to be developed on 574.2 acres of mountainside land along Business Route 6 and Wildcat Road. The plans call for 14 two-story buildings, each with a 202,340-square-foot footprint. The land is currently zoned for resource conservation and medium- to high-density residential use, and the project would proceed through a data center overlay zone rather than a full rezoning. The site is located adjacent to an existing electric substation, however the proposal also includes two on-site substations measuring 326,000 square feet and 69,000 square feet, respectively. This project would use an adiabatic closed-loop system, and additional infrastructure would include a 3.75-million-gallon water storage tank, a 33,000-square-foot administration building, and 588 diesel generators. This proposal is the largest in the Scranton-area, and is anticipated to have a capacity of 1.6 GW, which is greater than the working capacities of any single nearby power plant.

name: Project Gravity
address: Between Business Route 6 and Eynon-Jermyn Road
info: Archbald 25 Developer LLC (affiliated with Western Hospitality Partners) has proposed a hyperscale data center campus on 195 acres in Archbald. This campus would include at least seven two-story data center buildings, each with a 138,000 sq ft footprint, and entrances on both Business Route 6 and Eynon Jermyn Road. A new 230 kV substation is also planned. The developer has proposed similar projects in Indiana and Illinois, and the site falls under Archbald’s planned data center overlay. The project has been controversial due to the displacement of residents at the adjacent Valley View Estates (Jermyn Mobile Home Village), which entered into a sales agreement with Archbald Developer II LLC on May 21, 2025, with transfer expected April 15, 2026. Roughly three dozen residents — many on fixed incomes — now face relocation, and county officials have pledged assistance. The mobile home park lies just a few hundred feet from the proposed buildings, with the main campus entrance paralleling the park’s access road. The developer, linked to multiple data center efforts in Archbald and Blakely, is expanding rapidly across Lackawanna County. As of May 2026, Project Gravity has been removed from Pennsylvania's "Fast Track", a program that accelerates permitting for major economic development and infrastructure projects.

name: Blakely AI Data Center
address: Below Business Route 6 and Terrace Drive
info: Blakely Borough officials have responded to the withdrawn AI data center proposal by advancing a zoning amendment that will tightly regulate any future projects of that kind. The Borough Council voted unanimously to advertise the amendment, which confines data centers to a specific overlay district in the northwest corner of town above Business Route 6 and west of Scott Road, extending to the borough’s border with Dickson City and Scott Township. The amendment makes data centers conditional uses within that district and imposes nearly three dozen requirements before approval, including environmental, health, and sound studies—covering both audible and low-frequency noise—conducted at the developer’s expense by qualified professionals selected by the borough. Data centers will be limited to 40 feet in height, prohibited from generating on-site power, and required to occupy at least 20 acres with setbacks of 300 feet from property lines and roads and 200 feet from water bodies or wetlands. Developers must receive electrical power exclusively from Blakely Borough Electric Light Company, reimburse the borough for all related engineering and service expenses, allow quarterly inspections, and participate in emergency response training while supplying any necessary fire-suppression equipment. Other requirements include implementing water-conservation technologies such as closed-loop cooling systems, submitting all state and federal environmental permits, and following strict fencing, lighting, and vegetation standards to minimize visual impact. The amendment also defines data centers and related infrastructure, regulates energy-storage facilities, and adds zoning for short-term rentals. A public hearing on the ordinance is scheduled for November 17 at the Borough Building, with council expected to vote immediately afterward. The developers of the proposed Blakely Data Center have withdrawn their application for a zoning change after being denied the chance to present revised plans at a public meeting. While the data center itself will no longer move forward, the property will still be developed under existing zoning rules. Planned work includes tree removal, construction of a meadow-style pad for material storage, and site remediation. The developers expressed regret over missed opportunities from the canceled data center project, which they said could have generated over $119 million annually in fees for Blakely Electric, more than $2.2 million in local property taxes, created around 500 construction jobs, offered permanent high-paying positions, and funded environmental improvements such as landfill removal and mine reclamation. A local businessman had sought to rezone roughly 121 acres in Blakely from R-1 low-density residential to allow an AI data center campus with four two-story, 70-foot-tall buildings totaling about 300,000 square feet and requiring up to 1.5 GW of power—equal to the output of the Lackawanna Energy Center. The site was once planned for residential and commercial use and would have been the fifth data center proposal in the Valley View School District. The land lies within a federal opportunity zone and is eligible for $7 million in mine land reclamation funding. Blakely operates its own electric distribution system and continues to evaluate feasibility for potential future development.

name: Project Boson
info: Project Boson, formerly known as the Archbald Data & Energy Center, is separate from Project Gravity and will replace the Highway Auto Parts junkyard on Eynon Jermyn Road with a single, nearly 620,000-square-foot data center, according to recent land development filings. The project was initially planned as three two-story buildings but was later merged into one larger facility. It is led by PDC Realty LLC, managed by Jim Marzolino of Kriger Construction, who also signed the original zoning permit in April 2025 and a subsequent subdivision application in August. Marzolino, connected to both Project Boson and Project Gravity, plans to sell the Gravity site to Western Hospitality Partners under an October 2024 purchase agreement. The 83.5-acre Boson site includes a planned 200-foot tree buffer between the facility and nearby residential areas.

name: Jessup Breaker Street Data Center
info: Breaker Street Associates LLC (linked to Catalyst Commercial Development via address) has proposed a six-building data center campus totaling about 1.07 million square feet on a 131-acre site between Hill Street and the Casey Highway. Each building would rise up to 130 feet, with the campus projected to consume around 600 megawatts of power. Because the application was filed before Jessup adopted its new data center overlay zoning, the project is grandfathered under the older rules. However, a zoning appeal is underway concerning infrastructure planned in a residential zone—specifically a switch yard the borough’s zoning officer deemed non-compliant. The developer argues it should be permitted as either an accessory use or a public utility facility. In addition, the project will require a height variance since Jessup zoning limits buildings in the mixed-use district to 40 feet. On January 19th, the Jessup Zoning Hearing Board unanimously rejected the developer's appeal for zoning relief, but the developer has appealed the decision as of early February, hence this proposal is listed as delayed and not withdrawn.

name: Project Gold
address: Near I‑380 at Clifton Beach Road
info: The Project Gold data center campus, proposed by 1778 Rich Pike LLC, would develop a data center campus on roughly 1,000 acres straddling Clifton and Covington Townships. Initial plans called for nearly three dozen data centers, though revised plans show five larger buildings in Clifton ranging from 160,000 to nearly 685,000 square feet each. Gouldsboro Land LLC, linked to NE Edge Data Centers, has acquired more than 300 contiguous acres along Interstate 380 in Covington Twp., where the land falls within a newly created data center, energy and technology district. The project is currently tied up in court after a judge vacated a settlement agreement between the developer and Clifton Twp., following objections from the Clifton Twp. Zoning Hearing Board, which had unanimously denied the developer's challenge that local zoning unlawfully excluded data centers. This proposal remains very controversial.

name: East Whiteland Township Data Center
address: 13 South Bacton Hill Rd and Swedesford Rd
info: Sentinel Green Fig, LLC has proposed constructing two data center buildings on a roughly 70-acre site in East Whiteland Township at the former Foote Mineral Company property along S. Bacton Hill Road, near Swedesford Road. The site, which has been fully remediated after its prior Superfund designation, was previously approved for but never developed as a retirement community. The applicant proposes two data center buildings of ~410,000 square feet each, totaling ~828,000 square feet of building footprint. Primary access would be from Swedesford Road, with secondary emergency access to S. Bacton Hill Road, and the site would be served by public water and sewer.

name: QTS Salem Data Center
address: Adjacent to the Susquehanna Steam Electric Station
info: QTS and Blackstone Infrastructure, in partnership with PPL Corporation, announced a $25 billion plan to build several AI-oriented hyperscale data center campuses throughout Northeastern Pennsylvania. Sites will be co-located with dedicated gas-fired power plants, with operations targeted around 2030. Only one QTS site proposal has been announced so far, adjacent to the Susquehanna Steam Electric Station in Salem Township. The initial proposal included 18 buildings across three construction phases on an approximately 1,800-acre campus, but a revised master site plan was submitted in early 2026 reducing the total building count and relocating some structures. Notable changes include the removal of three buildings near the southern end of the western side of Walker Run, the removal of two buildings from the second-phase area, and the removal of two buildings from the northern third-phase section — though the three remaining northern buildings appear to be roughly double the size of those in the original plan. In March 2026, it was reported that QTS (through holding company Salem Township Holdings LLC) spent over $580 million acquiring approximately 1,700 acres from 97 local landowners. The project buildout is expected to span five to seven years.

name: AWS Keystone Trade Center Campus
address: Keystone Trade Center (former U.S. Steel Fairless Works)
info: Amazon announced plans to develop a "digital infrastructure campus" at the Keystone Trade Center in Fairless Hills as part of a statewide investment. The property is 1800 acres and was once owned by U.S. Steel.

name: CoreWeave Lancaster AI Campuses
companies: Machine Investment Group; Chirisa Technology Parks; CoreWeave
info: CoreWeave, in partnership with Blue Owl Capital, Chirisa Technology Parks, and Machine Investment Group, is advancing a multi-hundred-megawatt, multibillion-dollar (~6B committed by CoreWeave) AI data center project in Lancaster at the former RR Donnelley/LSC Communications sites at Greenfield Road and 1375 Harrisburg Pike, respectively. Construction is underway at Greenfield with expansion phases and the Harrisburg Pike campus is still in planning stages. Full buildout could support up to ~300 MW of capacity.

name: Allentown Warehouse Conversion Data Center
info: Langan Engineering is proposing an approximately quarter-million-square-foot data center at the site of a previously approved warehouse project from 2022. As of June, the developer has requested postponement of the proposal review, hence this entry is considered delayed.

name: PAX – Pennsylvania Digital I
address: Multiple campuses planned in the Carlisle area (exact parcels TBD)
companies: PowerHouse Data Center; Pennsylvania Data Center Partners
info: PowerHouse Data Centers and Pennsylvania Data Center Partners announced a $15 billion joint venture, Pennsylvania Digital I (PAX), comprising three hyperscale campuses around Carlisle, delivering 1.35 GW of capacity with expandability up to 1.8 GW across ~700 acres. Each of the three campuses features six buildings and a dedicated 450 MW substation. As of early 2026, the project has cleared key milestones: water and sewer rights were secured in January 2026 via a $14.1 million agreement with Middlesex Township, and on March 4, 2026, the Middlesex Township Board of Supervisors unanimously approved construction of the first 450 MW substation, clearing the way for Phase 1-b construction of the first data center to begin. The project is expected to generate more than $65 million in direct tax revenue, with over $45 million allocated to the Cumberland Valley school district and local community.

name: TECfusions “Keystone Connect” AI Campus
info: TECfusions acquired ~1,395 acres from Alcoa to build a 3GW AI-ready data center campus with on-site gas generation and microgrid.

name: Springdale AI Data Center Redevelopment
info: Developers proposed a ~180 MW, 565,000-square-foot AI data center to redevelop the retired Cheswick coal plant. After months of review and mixed community response, Springdale Borough Council voted 5–2 to approve the project via a conditional use permit, as data centers are not explicitly addressed in the borough’s zoning code. In mid-December, the developer requested six zoning variances to exceed existing dimensional regulations and all were approved.

name: Homer City Energy + AI Campus
companies: Homer City Redevelopment; Kiewit; GE Vernova
info: A partnership between Homer City Redevelopment and Kiewit proposes turning the existing 2 GW facility into a 4.5 GW natural gas power plant, paired with a large AI/HPC data center campus on the 3,200-acre former coal site. GE Verona will supply seven high-efficiency hydrogen-enabled 7HA.02 gas turbines, with first deliveries expected in 2026, and the site is expected to begin producing power by 2028 (previously 2027). Most recently, environmental groups and other organizations have raised concerns about the already-permitted project. Pennsylvania's DEP issued an air quality permit in November 2025 well ahead of schedule, drawing criticism from environmental advocates who say carbon and toxic air pollution from the site could harm nearby residents. According to Homer City Redevelopment, the project will deliver up to 4.5 GW of power to AI-driven hyperscale data centers while reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 60–65% per megawatt hour compared to the former coal plant, and supply power to thousands of homes on the local grid. It will create more than 10,000 direct on-site construction jobs and approximately 1,000 permanent positions in technology, operations, and energy infrastructure, backed by an initial capital investment exceeding $10 billion, making it the largest such investment in Pennsylvania's history. Construction began as of early April 2026.

name: Panther Creek (Nesquehoning) HPC/AI Conversion
address: Panther Creek Energy Facility site (approx. 101 Panther Creek Rd)
info: Bitfarms and T5 announced intent to repurpose the Panther Creek waste-coal facility into an HPC/AI data center, leveraging existing ~1 GW infrastructure. Bitfarms has since converted its $300 million debt facility with Macquarie Group into project-specific financing for a 350 MW HPC and AI campus at the site, drawing $100 million to begin equipment purchases and site work in late 2025, with $200 million remaining for construction.

name: Fairview Crossroads Data Center
address: Approx. off I-83, I-76, & Lewisberry Rd
info: Fairview Crossroads LLC proposes a ~750,000 sq ft, ~$500M data center in Fairview Township. On June 28, the Board of Supervisors approved a zoning ordinance change to allow data centers as a conditional use.

name: Dauphin Highlands Golf Course sold for Data Center
info: The Dauphin County General Authority approved the $45.6 million sale of Dauphin Highlands Golf Course in Swatara Township to a real estate group, Provident Realty, planning to build a data center. While officials emphasized that the sale will pay off the course’s $13 million debt and deliver long-term benefits to county residents, many community members voiced concerns about noise, water use, high utility costs, and limited job creation from the proposed development. The course, which has operated at a deficit since county ownership began in 1993, will remain open until the tentative sale closing date of December 31, 2027. The sale also includes adjoining land in Steelton, and while the authority argued the purchase price was far above recorded land value, nearby residents and golfers expressed disappointment over the loss of one of the region’s best public courses. As of March 2026, Provident purchased 11 more parcels of land in the area valuing approximately $50 million.

name: Hollenback Township Data Center Development Zoning Application
address: Properties off West County Hollenback Rd
info: Iron City is possibly developing a data center at the Iron City No. 1 Power Generator and Compute Center, somewhere in the Karthaus area. The PA Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) approved Iron City Wells, LLC to construction 15 natural gas–fired engine generators to "power a compute facility", which still does not have much information public.

name: Jessup Sunnyside Data Center
address: Directly next to Lackawanna Energy Center
info: Catalyst Commercial Development, through Sunnyside Road Associates, has shifted its plans in Jessup from a warehouse to a nearly 487,000-square-foot data center on a 48.3-acre parcel along Sunnyside Road, directly beside Invenergy’s Lackawanna Energy Center. The project, which mirrors the footprint of a previously approved 334,800-square-foot warehouse but will remain under 50 feet in height, is slated to begin construction on September 1, 2026, and finish a year later. Catalyst purchased the land in 2024 for $3.4 million from Pompey Coal Company, which also owns nearby property tied to other Catalyst proposals. In early June 2026, Jessup's council voted 3-2 to approve the project's preliminary plans. No end user has been specified.

name: McKees Rocks Industrial Site Data Center
address: Rock Point Industrial Site along Nichol Avenue
info: Officials in Stowe Township are considering a proposal to allow a future data center at the Rock Point Industrial site along Nichol Avenue, a strategically located riverside property. The plan is still in early stages, and current township ordinances do not permit data center operations, prompting commissioners to vote to advertise a potential ordinance change to gather public input before any decision. Township leaders view the proposal as a possible boost for jobs, tax revenue, and local economic growth, while some residents have raised questions about the project’s purpose, environmental impact, and energy consumption. Officials have stated that agencies such as the DEP and EPA would regulate environmental and safety concerns. The township board is expected to revisit and possibly vote on the proposal at its next meeting on November 10.

name: Project Hummingbird
address: Former Robena Mine site along the Monongahela River
info: A 1,400-acre hyperscale data center is proposed for Greene County at the former Robena Mine site along the Monongahela River. The project is led by Essential Utilities and International Electric Power of Pittsburgh and aims to go online in early 2029 and create 1,000+ construction jobs. On-site natural gas turbines will generate about 910 MW of power, supported by Eos Energy battery storage and grid connection for near-100% reliability. An 18-million-gallon-per-day water treatment facility will use Monongahela River water for cooling and power generation needs, with oversight by the DEP. Developers say the project will deliver energy efficiency and economic growth, positioning Pennsylvania as a national hub for AI infrastructure.

name: Zediker Station Data Center
address: Near the Interstate 79 and Interstate 79 corridors
info: CNX and JLL are collaborating to market and lease the 1,500-acre Zediker Station site in South Strabane Township, positioning it as a major data center development opportunity. The site offers 400 buildable acres with access to natural gas, water, and Remediated Mine Gas (RMG) that can support carbon-neutral power generation. JLL highlights the area’s strategic location between Ohio and Virginia data markets and its sustainable energy potential. Local officials emphasize the project’s economic promise, including new jobs and infrastructure investment.

name: Archbald I LLC Data Center Campuses
address: Between Business Route 6 and Eynon Jermyn Road
info: Archbald 1 LLC, associated with Provident Realty, submitted two applications in December 2025 to build a total of 22 data center buildings across two campuses flanking Project Gravity. The larger south campus, sometimes called "Project Scott", is 400.76 acres, located directly next to Ed Staback Memorial Park and Archbald Pothole State Park. According to the "Conditional Use Application Narrative" (see references for application), "each building is designed and proposed to have an overall floor area of approximately 154,850 square feet per level, two levels (i.e., 309,700 square feet total both levels), and an overall height of up to approximately 90 feet." Furthermore, "each building is designed and proposed to include an adjoining approximately 74,970-square-foot generator yard." Three electrical substations are also planned on the northern portion of the facility, near the transmission lines. The smaller campus near the Archbald–Jermyn border is proposed to have four approximately 150,000-square-foot buildings on a 2.9-million-square-foot (≈66-acre) lot with about 1.5 million square feet of paved area (see Oct 31, 2025 article from The Times-Tribune). Together, these two campuses sit on either side of Project Gravity’s seven-building data center campus proposal. The two Archbald I LLC campuses together would occupy roughly 466.76 acres (400.76-acre south campus + ~66-acre north campus) based on the attached sources. On March 27th, the Archbald Borough Council held a special meeting where they unanimously voted to deny Archbald I LLC's conditional use application for "Project Scott" / Archbald I LLC South campus—all one day before the March 28th decision deadline. Despite this denial, on April 24th, the developer issued a public notice in The Times-Tribune stating that Project Scott's application was approved automatically via sections 913.2(b)(2) and 908(1.2) of the Pennsylvania Municipalities Planning Code, 53 P.S. §§ 10913.2(b)(2), 10908(1.2), and section 1417.A of the Borough of Archbald's Zoning Ordinance. Now, some entity, whether it be the borough, a group, or a resident, must legally challenge the approval within 30 days (deadline: May 24th) to fight the decision.

name: Former Air Products Headquarters Data Center
address: Between Hamilton Blvd and Centronia Rd
info: Air Products have submitted plans for a large data center on a 194-acre site in Upper Macungie Township, reflecting a broader shift in the Lehigh Valley from mega-warehouse projects to data centers, but the proposal remains in limbo as Air Products conducts due diligence on whether the land is suitable for that use. The site was previously approved for three warehouses that never moved forward, and while the township has agreed to delay proceedings until February, some residents have expressed frustration and officials have made clear no further extensions will be granted. As of late May, the Upper Macungie Zoning Hearing Board rejected the appeal by Air Products, essentially haulting the proposal.

name: Pennhurst Data Center
info: Pennhurst Holdings LLC has proposed a new project to convert the former Pennhurst State School and Hospital property into a 1.3-million-square-foot data center campus. The application and preliminary site sketches detail five two-story data center buildings, a potential solar field, and an electrical substation. Township officials have confirmed that data centers are permitted under the property’s mixed-use-industrial zoning. As of mid February, the East Vincent Township Planning Commission recommended the township's board of supervisors deny the project, siting "technical deficiencies" and a failture to comply with the township's zoning ordinance. In mid March, the developer resubmitted their conditional use application, which includes an increase of data center square footage from the originally planned 1.3M to 1.9M and adds power generation. \n As of May 22, 2026, the East Vincent Board of Supervisors voted (3-0) to reject the conditional use application, citing non-compliance with zoning issues, unaddressed environmental concerns, and impact to quality of life. It is likely a legal battle will ensue due to the rejection.

name: Conshohocken Data Center
info: In late 2025, a proposal to build a large data center at the former Cleveland-Cliffs steel mill site along the Schuylkill River was set for a standard zoning hearing, with the developer seeking approval for a two-million-square-foot project. Residents and advocacy groups had come prepared to raise concerns, but the plans were abruptly withdrawn less than an hour into the hearing after it was revealed the developer lacked legal standing to apply, as its sale agreement for the property had not been finalized. Township officials expected the developer to refile, which would restart the hearing process the following year. \n As anticipated, in May 2026 the developer refiled an application to the zoning board detailing 10 buildings, some potentially with two floors, totaling approximately two million square feet of data center space, while the building footprints themselves would occupy, in total, roughly one million square feet.

name: Storage Data Center Project
info: Next Generation Land is advertising 700 acre site for hyperscale data center development somewhere in Tioga County. According to the developer, the site combines shovel-ready land with access to over 500,000 Dth/day of firm natural gas capacity, capable of supporting more than 2.5 GW of scalable power generation, with flexibility for both behind-the-meter and grid-connected strategies. Data center activity prompted Tioga County commissioners to proactively draft an ordinance, adopted in December 2025, addressing lighting, sound and noise, energy consumption, water consumption, landscape appearance, and future decommissioning. Development has faced notable community opposition, with residents expressing concerns primarily about not wanting a data center in the county at all. No definitive development plans have been finalized.

name: Dickson City Data Centers
address: Above Cold Spring Road, nearby McDonald's
companies: Dickson City Development LLC; Kriger Construction Inc
info:
A developer linked to multiple regional data center projects has submitted four applications to build four data centers on undeveloped mountainside land across multiple properties above Business Route 6 in Dickson City, spanning approximately 1.5 miles from the Blakely border toward Scranton. The proposal, filed by Dickson City Development LLC—affiliated with Kriger Construction—features multiple sites: three two-story buildings, each with 147,000-square-foot footprints, and a fourth 107,820-square-foot data center on land owned by Fore Up Realty, above Cold Spring Road. The three buildings are across multiple parcels, including two on adjacent parcels owned by the Circle Drive-In and HFS Realty Inc. (1501 Albright Avenue), and another on land on Bell Mountain owned by Mountainview DC Realty LLC. 
 On February 12, Dickson City approved a zoning ordinance restricting data center development to manufacturing-zoned districts. On February 17, Dickson City's zoning officer denied all four applications, citing insufficient information and a zoning ordinance for data centers that prohibited their development on commercially zoned land above Business Route 6. For context, Dickson City only has approximately 7–8 viable acres zoned for manufacturing, located on the opposite side of town, so this decision had the potential to stop development entirely. However, on April 29, at a Dickson City zoning hearing, an attorney representing Dickson City Development LLC appealed the denial. 
 Residents are worried about the Circle Drive-In Theater potentially selling, though its president stated at the meeting that there are no current plans to do so. 
 As of May 20, 2026, Dickson City zoning officials rejected (3-0) Dickson City Development LLC's zoning challenge to invalidate Dickson City's previous decision to only allow data center development in a light manufacturing district. This essentially rejects the proposals, however considering it is not a full plan withdrawal, more legal battles may be under way.

name: West Hazleton Data Center
info: One Trinity Real Estate Investments plans to market land behind Valmont Shopping Plaza in West Hazleton for a data center, citing existing industrial zoning, prior approval for a 600,000-square-foot building, and available utilities. The borough zoning officer denied the initial application because data centers are not explicitly permitted under current zoning, prompting a planning commission public hearing scheduled for Sept. 23 and potential further review by the zoning board. Separately, One Trinity is exploring redevelopment options for the shopping plaza itself, including renovations, partial demolition, or an entirely different use.

name: Sugarloaf Township Data Center
info: Chicago-based LBT Investment Group LLC has submitted a data center proposal in Sugarloaf Township, amid another unrelated proposal for a 12-mile PPL transmission line that has drawn public criticism. Sugarloaf Township previously denied LBT’s request, prompting the company to pursue legal action and negotiations with township officials, which ultimately led to proposed amendment to the township's zoning ordinance. The township counsel indicated that zoning law requires municipalities to allow and regulate all types of land uses, even those that are unpopular.

name: Linfield Data Center
info: Limerick Town Center LLC proposed and secured approval for a land swap with the Pennsylvania Game Commission that will transfer 55 acres of State Game Lands 234 in Limerick Township and a 200-foot right-of-way to the developer in exchange for 614 acres of company-owned land across three counties, including parcels in Limerick and Bern townships and 177 acres in Edgmont Township that will become Delaware County’s first state game lands. Despite opposition from residents and a formal objection from the Limerick Township Board of Supervisors, the Game Commission approved the exchange in a 6–3 vote in Harrisburg, clearing the way for development on the Heavy Industrial–zoned site adjacent to the former Publicker distillery, where uses could include data centers. As of mid-April 2026, it was publicized that the developer finally submitted an application for a 1.7-million-square-foot data center only the original property, making the purpose of the land swap unclear.

name: Kline Township Data Center
address: "Devils Mountain", bounded by Rt-309, I-81, and the villages of Lofty and Haddock
info: Amazon Web Services has purchased approximately 350 acres on "Devil's Mountain" to construct a data center campus consisting of 10 buildings. This site was previously proposed to be developed for several warehouses, but was later resold.

name: Ransom Township Data Center
info: Scranton Materials LLC has requested a zoning amendement from Ransom Township to allow for the construction of six data centers, each with a footprints of 156,648 square feet, on Scranton Materials' property, a stone quarry site. On Jan 20th, Ransom Township's planning commission held a meeting where they reviewed and rejected the developer's appeal for zoning relief, but as of early March, the developer has filed two separate legal challenges against the township over this ruling.

name: Atlas Industrial / South Whitehall Township Hyperscale Data Center Campus
address: 2493 N. Cedar Crest Blvd; across the street from Parkland High School
info: CDE Acquisitions LLC is proposing a 6-building, 5.1-million-square-foot data center campus on ~410 acres of undeveloped industrial-zoned land across the street from Parkland High School. The project is currently under review with multiple agencies, including PPL, PA Department of Environmental Protection, PA Department of Transportation, and the Lehigh County Authority.

name: Smithfield Gateway Project
address: Off Seven Bridge Rd and Independence Rd
info: A developer has proposed a multi-building project consisting of data center facilities, an electrical substation, a hotel, and restaurant uses on approximately 91.96 acres of land. A submitted sketch plan illustrates the anticipated future land development for Phases 1B, 2A, and 2B of the Smithfield Gateway project. The plan proposes two data center buildings of approximately 125,000 square feet each (approximately 500,000 square feet of gross floor area in total), a power substation, a 110-room hotel, and two standalone restaurant buildings. The project would be developed across parcels 16.8.2.7 and 16.8.2.7-2, as roughly depicted by the project's map boundary.

name: Project Phoenix
address: Adjacent to the upcoming Shippingport Power Station
info: Frontier Group of Companies is transforming the Bruce Mansfield Power Plant into the Shippingport Power Station, a 3.6GW natural gas power facility with a colocated data center, representing a $10 billion investment. TThe campus is planned to span roughly 1.8 million square feet across two to three buildings. According to a press release from The Frontier Group of Companies, the project is expected to create over 15,000 construction jobs, 340 new full-time jobs, and generate $6 billion in new economic activity in the region.

name: Bradford County / Klondike Data Center Project
info: A data center is planned for colocation with New Fortress Energy's Wyalusing Energy Center, an upcoming natural gas power facility. This facility will be located somewhere on a 335-acre site in Wyalusing Township, owned by New Fortress Energy/Klondike, which offers over 150 developable acres. Notably, this project operates under a unique architecture: the natural gas power facility will not be connected to the regional PJM electric grid, but will instead directly power on-site data center operations.

name: Limerick / Project Laurel Data Center
address: Across from Philadelphia Premium Outlets, near the Limerick Nuclear Power Plant
info: An undisclosed developer, MCD 7 LLC, has proposed a 1.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center campus on approximately 192 acres in Limerick Township, across from the Philadelphia Premium Outlets and near the Limerick Generating Station, a nuclear power plant. The plan includes eight 85-foot-tall data center buildings (five at 154,000 square feet, and three at 202,000 square feet) along with two 35-foot-tall administrative buildings, power substations, and secured fencing. The future tenant has not been disclosed due to a non-disclosure agreement. PECO, a gas and utility company, has agreed to supply up to 750 megawatts of power. According to the sources below, each data center building would include 30–45 backup generators and a similar number of rooftop chillers, and would employ 15–20 workers across three shifts. Full buildout is expected to take three to five years, pending completion of the township’s conditional use, zoning, and land development approval processes. The project is projected to generate $10 million to $20 million in annual tax revenue once fully built, primarily benefiting the Spring-Ford School District, and must comply with strict local data center zoning requirements related to noise, setbacks, and woodland preservation.

name: Peach Bottom Data Center
address: On site of York 2 Energy Center
info: Peach Bottom will host a $5 billion data center project on the site of the York 2 Energy Center. Not much information has been made public about this proposal yet.

name: NorthPoint Spring Mountain Data Center
info: NorthPoint Development has purchased hundreds of acres of land for a proposed data center around Spring Mountain. No specific details have been released about the site; anticipate updates as more information is released.

name: Penn Forest Township Data Center
info: Mele Brothers Realty has expressed interest in using their ~750 acres of commerically-zoned land off Rt 903 and Maury Rd for data center development. As of early March 2026, no applications have been filed, and Penn Forest Township supervisors have approved a zoning ordinance for data centers, requiring developers to meet specific noise, height, setback, etc. conditions to receive project approval.

name: Tobyhanna Township / Blakeslee Data Center
address: Pennsylvania Glacial Till property along Caughbaugh Road
info: Tobyhanna Township supervisors approved a zoning overlay allowing a data center on a 520-acre area called Glacial Till/Tarheel Quarry. This land is currently zoned rural residential and located near the Lehigh River, conservation areas, and wetlands. The ordinance permits data centers while imposing requirements such as large setbacks, minimum acreage, a closed-loop water system, noise limits of 70 decibels, and additional regulatory approvals. The overlay was adopted to meet zoning obligations and avoid legal challenges, despite resident concerns.

name: Talen Energy Rezone Request for Possible Data Center in Montour County
address: Between the Talen Energy Montour Power Plant and the Montour Preserve
info: Talen Energy has requested that Montour County rezone roughly 870 (originally 1300 according to sources) acres of mostly agricultural land in Anthony Township from agricultural to industrial use, citing potential future development that could include a large data center campus with approximately 12-15 buildings. In November, the Montour County Planning Commission voted against recommending the rezoning after determining that Talen’s plans were unclear, following public opposition from hundreds of residents concerned about the loss of prime farmland and potential impacts to water and community health. As of early February 2026, the Montour County Board of Commissioners has denied the rezoning request. Since then, Talen issued a statement saying they look forward to continued communication, and will provide more information on their plans for this site. Due to this, this proposal is considered delayed instead of withdrawn.

name: Fezzik Aliquippa Data Center
info: Five 9s Digital is marketing a 86-acre Pennsylvania site designed for a large-scale, natural gas–powered data center campus with up to 496 MW of power and on-site substations. The site is preapproved for behind-the-meter natural gas, supports an initial 250,000 SF data center expandable to 500,000 SF+, and allows further expansion across the property.

name: Dorrance Township Data Center Zoning Ordinance Challenge
address: Corner of South Main Rd and Yeager Rd
info: Brewster Land Company, LLC is challenging Dorrance Township’s zoning ordinance, arguing that the version in effect before a September amendment unlawfully excluded data centers as a permitted land use under Pennsylvania law. The company filed a substantive validity challenge with the zoning hearing board but has not yet submitted a formal application to build its proposed six-building data center on a 155-acre, commercially zoned parcel. October testimony focused on whether data centers were permitted or could be considered a conditional use under prior ordinance language, with township officials stating they were not expressly allowed. The hearing is bifurcated, and if the board rejects the challenge, the company may appeal to county court.

name: Project Green Mountain
address: East side of Casey Highway, at former Stavola Quarry site
info: Green Mountain 6, LLC has proposed a data center campus on former Stavola Quarry land above the Casey Highway. This campus will include seven two-story data center buildings with footprints of 138,000 SF (each building 276,000 GSF) with accessory back-up generator yards, a PP&L-owned switchyard, a customer substation, and extended detention basins. Due to similarities between Archbald's Project Green Mountain and Project Gravity, it is suspected that it that Green Mountain 6 is affiliated with Western Hospitality Partners, although this has not yet been confirmed.

name: East Manchester Farmland to Data Center Initiative Interest
address: York Baker Farm, off Long Rd and Gravel Hill Rd
info: East Manchester Township officials are planning zoning revisions for a 300-acre farm, York Baker Farm, to allow for data center development. Interest in this site has been expressed as it sits under two high-voltage power lines, and its vicinity to Brunner Island Power Station, which is already appropriately zoned for industrial use. Officials are drafting a zoning orindance to allow for data center development on the site, with public meeting expected on Jan 20th. On April 8th, 2026, a zoning ordinance was passed designating this land and a adjacent parcel as a data center overlay district.

name: Conewago Township Data Center Land Considerations
address: Properties along Bull Road and Mill Creek Road
info: Conewago Township officials are considering two rural areas for potential data center development: an agricultural wedding venue closing in 2026 on the western edge of the township and the Rexroth farm properties along the eastern border with Newberry Township. The Rexroth holdings total 541 acres and run along Bowers Bridge Road. Another set of properties under consideration includes an almost 130-acre parcel fronting Bull Road and bordering Dover Township, and a separate nearly 95-acre parcel on Mill Creek Road. Township planners are evaluating whether a data center overlay zone would apply to one or more of these locations.

name: Briar Creek Township Data Center Zoning Overlay
address: On Shickshinny Valley Road, below the Hemlock Springs development
info: Briar Creek Township supervisors are proposing a zoning overlay to allow for data center development on a 231-acre site across four parcels on Shickshinny Valley Road. The proposed overlay would apply to a site currently zoned for agricultural use, but would allow for data center development if approved. As reported on April 28th 2026 by Press Enterprise, a lawyer associated with the unnamed developer interested in building a data center in Briar Creek states that this 231-acre site is not ideal, and they prefer another site "on the north side of Knob Mountain and Shickshinny Valley roads, by state game lands." The developer expressed disappointment with several of the stipulations within the zoning overlay proposal, including height and decommissioning requirements set by the township.

name: South Hanover Township Data Center
address: Over Industrial zoning of Pennsy Supply
info: HCI DP Land Acquisition LLC has submitted two applications for two, one-story data center buildings. One application, concerning the Pennsy Supply quarry site, at 249.5 acres is currently zoned industrial. The second application relates to a legal challenge against the township's current zoning laws, which the developer claims is exclusionary of data center development and violates Pennsylvania law. The applications describe two data centers, one at 401,800 square feet and the other at 210,000 square feet, as well as a planned 210,000-square-foot substation. The developer has stated they anticipate approximately 120 full-time employees across both buildings.

name: Lower Mount Bethel Technology Center
address: Around the intersection of Gravel Hill Rd and Martins Creek Belvidere Highway
info: J.G. Petrucci Company and Peron Development are proposing a $5 billion data center campus on 450 acres at the intersection of Gravel Hill Rd and Martins Creek Belvidere Highway, across the road from the John M. Mauser Nature Education Trail System. Currently this land is zoned agricultural, which would require a rezone/zoning ordinance to proceed.

name: Sellersville / West Rockhill Township Data Center
address: 215 Cathill Rd, next to former Teva Pharmaceutical building
info: A developer presented rough plans for a 350,000-square-foot data center on approximately 40 acres of land next to the former Teva Pharmaceutical building in West Rockhill Township. The site is zoned for industrial use and is being marketed under a partnership between National Land Developers and Greener by Design. As of mid-April 2026, no formal application has been submitted to the township.

name: Iron City No. 1 Power Generator and Compute Center
info: Iron City is possibly developing a data center at the Iron City No. 1 Power Generator and Compute Center, somewhere in the Karthaus area. The PA Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) approved Iron City Wells, LLC to construction 15 natural gas–fired engine generators to "power a compute facility", which still does not have much information public.

name: North Coventry Township Data Center
info: Envision Land Use withdrew plans for a small "boutique" data center along Route 100 at 299 W. Schuylkill Road after the North Coventry Township Board of Supervisors voted to send Envision a note expressing disinterest in a data center at the proposed location, prior to a formal application being submitted. This proposal included a 120,000-square-foot, three story data center building that would employ six full-time workers.

name: Prologis Warehouse Data Center
companies: Prologis; Allen Township Board of Supervisors; Lehigh Valley Planning Commission
info: A data center project is planned at 2500 Liberty Drive in Allen Township, Northampton County, Pennsylvania. The site has a 1,011,808 square foot warehouse owned by Prologis, an industrial real estate investment trust. The building was approved for warehouse use in 2016 and completed in 2023, but never occupied as a warehouse. Prologis sought and received unanimous approval from the Allen Township Board of Supervisors on March 24, 2026 to convert it to a data center. The end tenant is undisclosed. Plans are in early phases, but the township meeting cited that the facility will use a closed-loop cooling system, has a privately funded electrical infrastructure including a private substation, and will incorporate solar panels. No capacity figures were disclosed publicly. They further cite that the project is expected to employ between 100 and 120 people.

name: Switch Data Center at Former Pitt Race Complex
info: Provident Data Centers (through its affiliate Wampum I LLC) purchased the former Pittsburgh International Race Complex ("Pitt Race") for $50 million to develop data centers. Owners Jim and Kathy Stout announced the sale of the over 400-acre site in October 2025, and the beloved racing facility closed its doors a month later. Switch has since announced plans for a 382-acre data center campus on that same property, with Provident as landowner and Switch as the campus developer and operator. Big Beaver Borough has confirmed Switch's intention to develop the former Pitt Race property at 201 Penndale Road, though the company has apparently not yet submitted a formal application.

name: Allenwood Data Center
info: PNK Group is proposing a 300 MW 4-building data center campus on land they own and developed at the Great Stream Commons. On site, a 69-kilovolt electric substation is also proposed to be built to support the data centers. Before this proposal can move forward, Gregg Township would need to amend its zoning ordinance to add a permittance to data centers in commercial and manufacturing zones.

name: Fort Cherry Development District
companies: Imperial Land Corp.; Liberty Energy Inc.; Imperial Land Corp.; Liberty Energy Inc.; Range Resources Corp.
info: Imperial Land Corp. is developing the Fort Cherry Development District, an 875-acre industrial and office park, with initial phases encompassing 2.3 million square feet of industrial, flex, and office space. The district makes direct use of the Southern Beltway (PA-576), providing quick access to Pittsburgh International Airport, the Shell Cracker Plant in Beaver County, and Interstate 79. As part of the district, a data center project announced in spring 2025 would be powered by a natural gas plant built and operated by Liberty Energy Inc., with fuel supplied by Range Resources Corp. Power would be delivered in 25 MW blocks up to a total capacity of 1 GW, with the overall data center footprint scaled to the number of clients at the site.

name: Starpointe Data Center Project
companies: Next Generation Land; Next Generation Land; Next Generation Land; Energy Transfer; Range Resources Corp.
info: Next Generation Land is developing an 800+ acre hyperscale data center campus at Starpointe Business Park. The site is fully zoned industrial with data centers permitted by right, with 120+ acres already cleared, graded, and pad-ready, with an active NPDES permit in place. Power would be supplied behind-the-meter by an on-site natural gas plant connected to Energy Transfer's Rover Pipeline, capable of delivering up to 3.5 GW of capacity, with natural gas sourced from Range Resources. The project is currently in the early stages of development.

name: Champion Data Center Project
companies: Next Generation Land; Next Generation Land; Next Generation Land; Next Generation Land; PJM Interconnection
info: Next Generation Land is developing Champion, a 1,000+ acre hyperscale data center campus at a former coal-refuse brownfield site with existing industrial zoning. The campus is supported by six nearby natural gas pipelines, two of which run on-property, with a potential capacity of approximately 5 GW. The site carries a proven 300+ MW PJM interconnection history, an active NPDES permit, multiple fiber routes, and a Keystone Opportunity Zone designation. The project is currently in the early stages of development.

name: South Annville Township Data Center
address: Along Mt. Pleasant Road, adjacent to 911 Rapid Response
companies: Inch & Co; Eastern Land & Resources Co
info: Inch & Co. and Eastern Land & Resources Co. are proposing a $1.7 billion data center on 99 acres adjacent to the 911 Rapid Response building in South Annville. The developer has stated that the proposal is still in the early conceptual stage, so exact capacity and water usage details are not yet known, however, five buildings are expected to be constructed, and they would emit between 45-55 decibels. This proposal also sits adjacent to a much larger industrial complex, DHL's Clear Spring Logistics Park, which will house multiple warehouses. The property is zoned I-1 (industrial) and C-1 (commercial), and the township has set out three requirements for the project. Among them, the conversion of 15 industrial acres to agricultural use, both to protect an eagle nest in the southwest corner of the property and to offset 13 acres being rezoned from agricultural to industrial. \n As of May 2026, the developers have withdrawn this proposal following considerable community opposition.

name: Newton Township Data Center
address: East Newton Ira Tripp Tract / E Newton IRA Trip Township Road L R
info: Popca LLC is proposing a $280 million, two-building data center campus on roughly 90 acres in across the road from another proposed data center in Ransom Township. Each building would stand 35 feet tall and span 145,200 square feet, with supporting infrastructure including generation yards, a substation, well, septic system, guard building, and stormwater facilities. The township's zoning officer denied the application in February, ruling that data centers are not a permitted use in Newton's rural resource zoning district. Popca LLC has since appealed, with their attorneys arguing the denial misapplies the zoning ordinance and that Pennsylvania law requires municipalities to zone for all possible uses. The appeal hearing is scheduled for April 27 at 7 p.m. at the Newton Township municipal building in Clarks Summit.

name: New Castle Data Center
address: By I-81 on the south and West Laurel Street on the north
info: KRNL Data Centers approached New Castle Township officials about rezoning an 89-acre conservation tract near Frackville to allow for data center construction. The property is currently owned by Mount Laurel Enterprises and sits behind Broad Mountain Health & Rehabilitation Center near Frackville's Laurel Street residential area. Though no formal plans have been filed, the proposed complex would reportedly consist of multiple buildings rising above the tree line. The decision on whether to approve the project would rests with New Castle Township supervisors.

name: Watts Township Data Center
companies: MRP Industrial; MRPI Amity Hall, LLC
info: MRP Industrial submitted an application to the Perry County Planning Commission in late March 2025 for a 10-building, 1.48-million-square-foot data center complex, on a 288-acre parcel of industrially-zoned land between Amity Road and Susquehanna Trail. This announcement arrives as the township was considering a zoning ordinance to add data centers as an accessory use in industrial zones while adding restrictions, like locations, and special requirements for approval. \n The Perry County Commission mentioned the plans describe six buildings at approximately 150,000 square feet each, and four buildings at approximately 140,000 square feet each. Previously, the developer had proposed a warehouse complex on the site, which was staled for several years, until in December 2025, when Watts Township supervisors declined to give the developer an extension to amend their previously rejected application.

name: Project Triboro
companies: Triboro Commerce Park, LLC; Sansone Group
info: Two separate developers are separately proposing to build 16 data centers in total east of the Casey Highway. \n First, in early April, Triboro Commerce Park LLC, a company associated with DeNaples, submitted a plan to develop 7 data centers on just over 400 acres of the roughly 1,000-acre CM-2 zone. In late April, the company also filed a land-use appeal and a petition for an emergency injunction in Lackawanna County Court of Common Pleas asking the court to overturn Olyphant Borough Council's decision declaring its zoning ordinance invalid via a curative amendment, citing the action unlawfully excludes data centers — a further signal of its intent to build. \n Then, on May 4, Olyphant received a zoning application from ERCOR Triboro, a limited liability company under Sansone Group, to change its land use from four warehouses to nine data centers. Their campus spans about 500 acres and is adjacent to DeNaples land.

name: Linde Clinton Data Center
info: Linde Corporation, a NEPA headquartered infrastructure contractor, is proposing to build a data center on a 743 acre plot the company owns in Clinton Township, directly south east of Forrest City. The company also owns an adjacent 17 acre plot over a portion of wetlands feeding into the Lackawanna River. The application was made on April 30th, the same day that the Board of Supervisors voted to enact a 6 month moratorium. The Planning Commission voted on May 7th to postpone their final decision until June because the original application came within 10 days of the meeting. The May 7th meeting was met with a large community presence, objecting to the application.

name: East Brandywine Data Center
info: 500 ER-EBY Trust likely intends to advertise/build a data center on their 25-acre property at 500 East Reeceville Road, which is currently zoned R-2 Residential. Since data centers aren't permitted anywhere in the township, the site owner is challenging the zoning ordinance and proposing an amendment that would formally define "data center" and allow one as a permitted use in the R-2 zone.

name: Renaissance Boulevard Data Center Campus
info: MLP Ventures is proposing a 5-building data center campus off Renassiance Road. This developer is connected to redevelopment proposal for the former Cleveland Cliffs steel plant in Plymouth Township.

name: 600 River Road Data Center
info: Innovation River Run LLC has submitted a preliminary plan for a 450,000-square-foot data center next to the Schyulkill River.

name: 3200 Horizon Drive Data Center
info: A developer has submitted a preliminary plan for a 370,000-square-foot data center at 3200 Horizon Drive, adjacent to an existing small data center at 3400 Horizon Drive.

name: Smithfield Data Center
companies: ReNew Developers LLC; Hanton City Investments; Revity Energy
info: Hanton City Investments, an entity created by two local families, has described a preliminary plan for a data center as part of a broader business park near the forested Hanton City area adjacent to Fidelity Investments' 380-acre campus. A data center is described as one possible tenant the group is considering to financially catalyze the business park, with a Warwick solar developer serving as spokesperson for the investment group. Uniquely, the site sits atop what a 2010 historic inventory described as a late 17th-century farming village, called "Hanton City", which has been  reduced to overgrown ruins and scattered stone walls on a large undeveloped tract of land. No formal application has been submitted to the town, and the Smithfield Planning Board voted in March 2026 to send a proposed data center zoning amendment to the Town Council, which will be brought up during an upcoming May 5th meeting.

name: Colossus
address: 3231 Paul R. Lowry Road, Frank C. Pidgeon Industrial Park
companies: CTC Property LLC; Phoenix Investors; xAI; Memphis Light Gas and Water (MLGW); Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA)
info: The xAI Colossus supercomputer is located at 3231 Paul R. Lowry Road in South Memphis, Tennessee, in a 785,000 square foot former Electrolux manufacturing facility situated on over 217 acres. An affiliate of Phoenix Investors acquired the building in December 2023. By March 2024 the property was leased to xAI. They took over the factory with the goal of building the world's largest supercomputer, named Colossus, to fuel artificial intelligence, with more than a dozen generators steadily burning methane gas visible at the site as early as September 2024. In November 2024, TVA approved xAI's request for over 100 MW of grid power supplied through MLGW, upgrading from an initial grid connection of only 8 MW (local news asserts Musk has said the site needs 150 MW). xAI submitted a contract to purchase 13 acres of City of Memphis-owned land at 2685 Plant Road, located on the south side of the T.E. Maxson Wastewater Treatment Facility, through its affiliate CTC Property LLC, for $820,000. The proposed Colossus Water Recycling Plant will process municipal wastewater from the T.E. Maxson Wastewater Treatment Plant with a planned capacity of 10.4 million gallons per day. TVA and Nucor Steel have signed Letters of Intent to purchase water from the facility alongside xAI, with peak demand by all three facilities estimated at 12.5 million gallons per day.

name: Colossus 2
info: This project shares its history with Colossus, and is an expansion of the initial site, located at 5420 Tulane Road. The facility, previously a 1,000,000 square foot warehouse owned by Coliers and built in late 2023. MZX TECH LLC, an affiliate of xAI, purchased the site in February 2025 for about $80 million. Based on satellite imagery and analysis of the cooling equipment, SemiAnalysis estimated Colossus 2 is progressing toward 400 MW of critical IT capacity, with a hybrid cooling system of approximately 130 dry coolers and 135 adiabatic cooling units, producing an estimated annual water footprint of approximately 346 million gallons per year (roughly 0.9 million gallons per day). By April 2025, aerial imagery showed 35 gas turbines operating at a combined 422 MW without permits, which the Southern Environmental Law Center stated makes the facility likely the largest industrial emitter of NOx in Memphis, setting a good estimate for its capacity.

name: ExoticRidge Bitcoin Mine
info: ExoticRidge is proposing a cryptomining facility adjacent to a gas fractionation plant in Bulls Gap. Beacon Center is currently representing ExoticRidge in a federal lawsuit that seeks to repeal a Hawkins County ban on data centers and cryptocurrency mines from late 2025. Because of this, the proposal is marked as "delayed."

name: CleanSpark Mountain City Bitcoin Mine
info: Mountain City Board passed the proposed data center's rezoning ordinance on their first reading. The puiblic responded negatively to this decision, prompting Mountain City to later deny the ordinance, which was necessary for CleanSpark to begin constructing the proposed 30 MW facility.

name: Gallatin Data Center Expansion
companies: Plateau Excavation LLC.; DPR Construction; Meta
info: In 2020, Meta began constructing their Gallatin data center campus, which consists of 12 data center buildings (called "data halls") and two administrative buildings. It has been online since 2024.

name: Neural Nexus Data Center
info: In December 2025, Legacy Power Capital announced "Neural Nexus," a a massive $9.4 billion 1,742,400-square-foot mixed-use tech district to-be located in downtown Memphis. Situated on two city blocks near FedExForum, it features a 125,000-square-foot cloud computing center for AI, along with residential units, a hotel, and retail space.

name: RadiusDC Nashville I / Trinity Hills Colocation Data Center
info: RadiusDC began constructing a data center in the Trinity Hills area in 2025, and is expected to open in 2026. This facility is small and only has a capacity of 12 MW. Marketed as the largest colocation facility in Nashville.

name: 5C Memphis Data Center
info: 5C began constructing a data center campus in 2024 at the former Fred's headquarters.
